crime: She was the prison's miracle: burns, fevers, split knees — she can draw them out through her palms into her own body, and she recovers after a night's sleep. She never worked it in private; a healing was a show, done where the room could watch.

She asked for help exactly once. At the card table she said she might not manage anymore; the girl across from her laughed, said "you? you'll be fine," and dealt the next hand. No one had ever taken a turn for her.
That night, locked in the cell she slept in alone, she climbed the bunk rail and dropped herself onto the stone floor — again and again, until something inside her broke. She lay with it until six.
At breakfast she crossed to the card player's table and took her hand — the healer's touch, the one gesture nobody questions. But the girl was dead before her tea cooled.

When a wound is moved, its age moves with it. The bruises on the body had grown for hours, on a girl seen whole at six, and their spread matched falls from a height the dining hall does not contain. A bunk rail is that height.
Bruises older than the morning mean they were moved from another body — and only one girl can do that.
fields.epitaph: Her whole life, she was waiting for her turn.
execution: An iron cradle sized for a girl rose on the execution stand.
Its arms lifted her in, wrapped her like an infant, and rocked her; an arm shaped like a nurse's fed her broth by the spoonful and wiped her mouth, while every prisoner stood and watched.
With each rock the swaddling drew tighter, until nothing in the cradle moved.

magic.name: Woundshift
magic.text: By touch, she moves a wound or a sickness out of one body and into another.
